u1_l1.1.title: Review of Algebra (factoring, radicals, rational expressions)

flashcards.definition: The largest factor shared by all terms; always factor it out first.
a² − b² = (a + b)(a − b).
Multiplying numerator and denominator by the conjugate to eliminate radicals from the denominator.
A fraction where the numerator and/or denominator are polynomials.
The smallest expression divisible by all denominators; used to add or subtract rational expressions.
Order of operations: Parentheses, Exponents, Multiplication/Division, Addition/Subtraction.
a^m · a^n = a^(m+n); when multiplying same bases, add exponents.
(a^m)^n = a^(mn); when raising a power to a power, multiply exponents.
a^(−n) = 1/a^n; a negative exponent means the reciprocal.
a^(m/n) = ⁿ√(a^m); the denominator is the root, the numerator is the power.
Any set of ordered pairs (x, y).
A relation where every input has exactly one output.
If any vertical line intersects a graph more than once, the graph is not a function.
A function where each output corresponds to exactly one input; passes the horizontal line test.
f(x) represents the output of function f for input x.
The set of all valid input values (x-values) for a function.
The set of all output values (y-values) produced by a function.
Uses parentheses (open) and brackets (closed) to describe sets: (a, b) excludes endpoints; [a, b] includes them.
Values that make a denominator zero or a radicand negative — they are not in the domain.
Combines two or more intervals: e.g., (−∞, 2) ∪ (2, ∞).
Notation for a function named f with input variable x; f(3) means evaluate f at x = 3.
Substituting a value for the input variable and simplifying.
[f(x+h) − f(x)] / h; measures the average rate of change over an interval of width h.
Substitute (a + h) for x in the function's formula; used in the difference quotient.
Check which condition the input satisfies, then use the corresponding formula.
The point where the graph crosses the x-axis; found by setting y = 0.
The point where the graph crosses the y-axis; found by evaluating f(0).
f(−x) = f(x); the graph is symmetric about the y-axis.
f(−x) = −f(x); the graph is symmetric about the origin.
f(a) < f(b) whenever a < b on an interval; the graph rises from left to right.
f(x) + c shifts up; f(x) − c shifts down.
f(x − c) shifts right c units; f(x + c) shifts left c units.
−f(x) reflects the graph across the x-axis (flip vertically).
f(−x) reflects the graph across the y-axis (flip horizontally).
a·f(x): |a| > 1 stretches vertically; 0 < |a| < 1 compresses vertically.
Apply g first, then apply f to the result; written as (f ∘ g)(x).
The function that undoes f: f(f⁻¹(x)) = x and f⁻¹(f(x)) = x.
Swap x and y in y = f(x), then solve for y.
Only one-to-one functions (passing the horizontal line test) have inverses.
The graph of f⁻¹ is the reflection of f's graph across the line y = x.
A function defined by different formulas on different intervals of its domain.
On a graph, indicates the endpoint is NOT included in that piece.
On a graph, indicates the endpoint IS included in that piece.
The function is continuous if the pieces connect at each boundary without gaps.
|x| = x if x ≥ 0; −x if x < 0.
A function or equation that represents a real-world relationship between variables.
y = mx + b; constant rate of change — used for steady growth, motion at constant speed, etc.
y = ax² + bx + c; parabolic — models projectile motion, area problems, profit optimization.
y = ab^t or y = ae^(rt); models growth/decay: populations, investments, radioactive decay.
1) Identify variables. 2) Choose function type. 3) Write equation. 4) Solve and interpret.
y = mx + b; m is the slope and b is the y-intercept.
y − y₁ = m(x − x₁); used when a point (x₁, y₁) and slope m are known.
m = (y₂ − y₁)/(x₂ − x₁); measures the steepness and direction of a line.
Ax + By = C; A, B, C are integers with A ≥ 0.
y = c is horizontal (slope 0); x = c is vertical (slope undefined).
Lines with equal slopes: m₁ = m₂ (and different y-intercepts).
Lines whose slopes are negative reciprocals: m₁ · m₂ = −1.
The point where the line crosses the x-axis; found by setting y = 0.
The point where the line crosses the y-axis; found by setting x = 0.
For Ax + By = C, the slope is m = −A/B.
f(x) = ax² + bx + c; vertex at x = −b/(2a).
f(x) = a(x − h)² + k; vertex at (h, k).
The vertical line x = h that passes through the vertex.
a > 0: parabola opens up (minimum); a < 0: opens down (maximum).
|a| large → narrow; |a| small → wide.
A technique to rewrite ax² + bx + c as a(x − h)² + k.
An expression of the form x² ± 2px + p² = (x ± p)².
The key step: take b/(2a), square it, add and subtract.

flashcards.term: Vertex Form from Completing the Square
flashcards.definition: The result a(x − h)² + k reveals the vertex (h, k).
Completing the square on ax² + bx + c = 0 yields x = (−b ± √(b²−4ac))/(2a).
x = (−b ± √(b²−4ac))/(2a); solves any quadratic equation ax² + bx + c = 0.
Δ = b²−4ac; determines the number and type of solutions.
i = √(−1); i² = −1. Used when the discriminant is negative.
If a + bi is a solution, then a − bi is also a solution.

flashcards.term: Two Real, One Repeated, or Two Complex
flashcards.definition: Δ > 0: two real; Δ = 0: one repeated; Δ < 0: two complex.
The highest or lowest point of a parabola; (h, k) in vertex form.
The vertical line x = h through the vertex; the parabola is symmetric about it.
f(0) = c in standard form; the point where the parabola crosses the y-axis.
Solutions to f(x) = 0; where the parabola crosses the x-axis.
Points equidistant from the axis of symmetry have the same y-value.
R(x) = price × quantity; often quadratic when price varies linearly with demand.
P(x) = Revenue − Cost = R(x) − C(x).
Finding the maximum or minimum of a function; for quadratics, use the vertex.
h(t) = −16t² + v₀t + h₀ (in feet); the vertex gives maximum height.
Where profit = 0 (revenue equals cost).
f(x) = a|x − h| + k; V-shaped graph with vertex at (h, k).
If c > 0: x = c or x = −c; if c = 0: x = 0; if c < 0: no solution.
Becomes −c < expression < c; a compound inequality.
Becomes expression > c OR expression < −c.
(h, k) in f(x) = a|x − h| + k; the point where the V changes direction.
When multiplying or dividing both sides by a negative number, reverse the inequality sign.
Solve ax² + bx + c > 0 (or <, ≥, ≤) by finding zeros and testing intervals.
The solutions to the corresponding equality; they divide the number line into intervals.
A tool that tracks the sign (+/−) of each factor in each interval to determine the overall sign.
Express solutions using unions and intervals: e.g., (−∞, 2) ∪ (5, ∞).
The highest exponent of x in the polynomial; determines the function's overall behavior.
The coefficient of the term with the highest degree; its sign affects end behavior.
A function of the form aₙxⁿ + … + a₁x + a₀ with non-negative integer exponents.
A point where the graph changes direction; a degree-n polynomial has at most n − 1 turning points.
Every polynomial of degree n ≥ 1 has exactly n zeros (real or complex, counting multiplicity).
The direction f(x) heads as x → +∞ and x → −∞; determined by degree and leading coefficient.
Both ends rise: as x → ±∞, f(x) → +∞.
Both ends fall: as x → ±∞, f(x) → −∞.
Falls left, rises right: as x → −∞ f(x) → −∞; as x → +∞ f(x) → +∞.
Rises left, falls right: as x → −∞ f(x) → +∞; as x → +∞ f(x) → −∞.
Divide leading terms, multiply, subtract, bring down, repeat; works for any polynomial divisor.
A shortcut for dividing by (x − c) using only coefficients; faster than long division.
When f(x) is divided by (x − c), the remainder equals f(c).
(x − c) is a factor of f(x) if and only if f(c) = 0.
f(x) = d(x)·q(x) + r(x); q is the quotient and r is the remainder.
A value c where f(c) = 0; corresponds to an x-intercept.
The number of times a factor (x − c) appears; affects how the graph behaves at x = c.
The graph crosses the x-axis at that zero.
The graph touches (bounces off) the x-axis at that zero without crossing.
A degree-n polynomial has exactly n zeros (real or complex, counting multiplicity).
If f is continuous on [a, b] and N is between f(a) and f(b), then f(c) = N for some c in (a, b).
A function with no breaks, holes, or jumps; polynomials are continuous everywhere.
When f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs, indicating a zero must exist between a and b.
IVT guarantees a zero exists in an interval but does not give its exact location.
A numerical technique using IVT repeatedly to narrow down the location of a zero.
Find intercepts, determine end behavior, note multiplicity at zeros, plot key points, sketch smoothly.
The point (0, f(0)); found by evaluating the polynomial at x = 0.
Points where f(x) = 0; found by factoring or using the quadratic formula.
Local maxima or minima where the graph changes direction; at most n − 1 for degree n.
Polynomial graphs are continuous with no sharp corners, cusps, or breaks.
Possible rational zeros p/q: p divides the constant term, q divides the leading coefficient.
The term with no x; its factors provide the numerators of possible rational zeros.
The coefficient of the highest-degree term; its factors provide the denominators.
Use substitution or synthetic division to check if a candidate p/q is actually a zero.
After finding one zero, divide it out and apply the theorem to the quotient.
Cutting equal squares from corners of a flat sheet and folding up to form a box; volume is a polynomial in the cut size.
The set of x-values that make physical sense in a real-world model (e.g., positive lengths).
R(x) = price × quantity; often modeled as a polynomial when price depends on quantity.
Fitting a polynomial to data points to model trends; degree chosen to balance accuracy and simplicity.
Finding the maximum or minimum value of a polynomial model, using the vertex, critical points, or technology.
C(x) = fixed cost + variable cost terms; a polynomial in units produced x.
The additional cost to produce one more unit; approximated by the derivative of the cost function.
s(t) = s₀ + v₀t + ½at²; a degree-2 polynomial when acceleration is constant.
Finding the point(s) where revenue equals cost: R(x) = C(x).
C̄(x) = C(x)/x; the cost per unit, which decreases then increases (economies of scale).
A ratio P(x)/Q(x) of two polynomials, where Q(x) ≠ 0.
x-values that make the denominator zero; the expression is undefined there.
The smallest expression divisible by every denominator; used when adding or subtracting rational expressions.
A fraction whose numerator or denominator (or both) contain fractions.
Factor numerator and denominator, cancel common factors, and state restrictions.
A vertical line x = a where the function → ±∞; occurs where the denominator is zero (after cancellation).
A horizontal line y = L that the function approaches as x → ±∞.
A linear asymptote y = mx + b that occurs when the numerator's degree is exactly one more than the denominator's.
n < m → y = 0; n = m → y = aₙ/bₘ; n > m → no HA.
The function increases or decreases without bound (→ +∞ or → −∞) on either side.
A point where a common factor cancels; the function is undefined but the limit exists.
A vertical asymptote where the function → ±∞; the limit does not exist finitely.
Occurs when left-hand and right-hand limits exist but are not equal.
A factor present in both numerator and denominator that, when canceled, creates a hole.
At x = c: substitute c into the simplified expression to find the y-value.
Found by setting the numerator equal to zero (after canceling common factors).
f(0) = (numerator evaluated at 0)/(denominator evaluated at 0), if defined.
Divide the number line at zeros and VAs; test the sign of f in each interval.
f(−x) = f(x); graph is symmetric about the y-axis.
f(−x) = −f(x); graph is symmetric about the origin.
Breaking a rational expression into a sum of simpler fractions whose denominators are factors of the original.
Each factor (x − a) contributes A/(x − a) to the decomposition.
(x − a)ⁿ contributes A₁/(x−a) + A₂/(x−a)² + … + Aₙ/(x−a)ⁿ.
A quadratic that cannot be factored over the reals; contributes (Ax + B)/(quadratic).
Degree of numerator < degree of denominator; required before decomposition.
Total distance divided by total time; when speeds vary, this is a rational expression.
C(x) = (amount of substance) / (total volume); models dilution and mixing.
1/time = rate of work; combined rate: 1/a + 1/b = 1/t.
C̄(x) = C(x)/x; cost per unit, which decreases with volume due to fixed costs.
A statement that two ratios are equal: a/b = c/d; solved by cross-multiplication.
Zeros of the numerator (where expression = 0) and zeros of the denominator (where expression is undefined).
A number line divided at critical numbers; test the sign of the expression in each interval.
For ≤ or ≥, include x-values where the expression equals 0 (numerator = 0).
Never include x-values where the denominator = 0 (always excluded).
Combine terms so one side is 0 and the other is a single rational expression.
R_total = R₁R₂/(R₁ + R₂) for two resistors in parallel.
A ratio of polynomials in s (or z) that describes the input-output relationship of a linear system.
Strength/stress; a ratio that must exceed 1 for safe design.
y = k/x; as one quantity increases, the other decreases.
Finding max/min of a rational function subject to engineering constraints.
f(x) = a · bˣ with a ≠ 0, b > 0, b ≠ 1; grows if b > 1, decays if 0 < b < 1.
The irrational constant ≈ 2.71828; base of the natural exponential function.
The time required for a quantity undergoing exponential decay to reduce by half.
The time required for a quantity undergoing exponential growth to double: T_d = ln 2 / k.
A(t) = A₀ · eᵏᵗ; k > 0 is growth, k < 0 is decay.
A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt); P = principal, r = rate, n = compoundings/yr, t = years.
A = Peʳᵗ; the limit of compound interest as compounding frequency → ∞.
The initial amount of money invested or borrowed.
Effective annual rate accounting for compounding: (1 + r/n)ⁿ − 1.
Approximate doubling time ≈ 72/r (where r is the percentage rate).
The inverse of exponentiation: log_b(y) = x means bˣ = y.
log_b(MN) = log_b(M) + log_b(N).
log_b(M/N) = log_b(M) − log_b(N).
log_b(Mⁿ) = n · log_b(M).
log base e; ln(x) = logₑ(x). Inverse of eˣ.
log_b(x) = log(x)/log(b) = ln(x)/ln(b); converts any log to base 10 or base e.
Most calculators only have log (base 10) and ln (base e); other bases require conversion.
log_b(x) = log_a(x) / log_a(b) for any valid base a > 0, a ≠ 1.
log_b(a) = 1 / log_a(b).
To find log₃(7): type ln(7)/ln(3) or log(7)/log(3).
If bˣ = bʸ and b > 0, b ≠ 1, then x = y.
To solve bˣ = c: x = ln(c)/ln(b) or x = log(c)/log(b).
If log_b(x) = k, then x = bᵏ. Converts log form to exponential form.
A solution from algebraic steps that doesn't satisfy the original equation.
log_b(x) requires x > 0; reject solutions that violate this.

u5_l5.6.title: Applications: pH, Richter Scale, Population Growth

flashcards.definition: pH = −log[H⁺]; measures acidity/basicity of a solution.
M = log(I/I₀); each integer increase = 10× amplitude increase.
dB = 10 log(I/I₀); a logarithmic measure of sound intensity.
P(t) = P₀eᵏᵗ; solve for t using ln to find when population reaches a target.
Convert multiplicative relationships to additive ones for easier comparison.
y = bˣ passes through (0, 1) with HA y = 0.
y = log_b(x) passes through (1, 0) with VA x = 0.
The exponential and log with the same base are reflections over y = x.
For y = a·b^(x−h) + k, the HA becomes y = k.
For y = a·log_b(x−h) + k, the VA becomes x = h.
P(t) = L/(1 + be⁻ᵏᵗ); models growth limited by carrying capacity L.
The maximum sustainable population; upper horizontal asymptote.
The characteristic shape of a logistic graph: slow start, fast middle, slow finish.
Occurs at P = L/2; the point where growth rate is maximum.
P(0) = L/(1 + b); set t = 0 to find the starting population.
N(t) = N₀ · 2^(t/d); population doubles every d time units.
C(t) = C₀ · e⁻ᵏᵗ; drug concentration decreases exponentially.
Uses the known half-life (~5730 yr) to estimate the age of organic material.
P = Ae⁻ʳᵗ; the current worth of a future sum under continuous discounting.
V(t) = V₀(1 − r)ᵗ; models the declining value of an asset over time.
The angle subtended by an arc equal in length to the radius; 2π rad = 360°.
1/360 of a full rotation.
Multiply by π/180.
Multiply by 180/π.
Angle with vertex at the origin and initial side on the positive x-axis.
s = rθ; θ must be in radians.
A = (1/2)r²θ; θ must be in radians.
The rate of angle change: ω = θ/t, measured in rad/s.
v = rω; the speed of a point on the circumference.
Both arc length and sector area formulas require the angle in radians.
Circle of radius 1 centered at the origin; point at angle θ is (cos θ, sin θ).
0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90° and their multiples; have exact trig values using √2 and √3.
All trig positive in Q I; Sine in Q II; Tangent in Q III; Cosine in Q IV.
The acute angle between the terminal side and the x-axis.
x = cos θ, y = sin θ for any angle θ.
sin = opp/hyp, cos = adj/hyp, tan = opp/adj; mnemonic for right triangle trig.
Reciprocal of sine: csc θ = 1/sin θ.
Reciprocal of cosine: sec θ = 1/cos θ.
Reciprocal of tangent: cot θ = cos θ/sin θ.
sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 for all θ.

u6_l6.5.title: Graphs of Trigonometric Functions (amplitude, period, phase shift)

flashcards.definition: |A| in y = A sin(Bx − C) + D; half the distance between max and min.
2π/|B|; the length of one complete cycle of sine or cosine.
C/B; horizontal displacement of the graph.
D; the midline of the graph shifts to y = D.
π/|B| for y = A tan(Bx − C) + D.
Returns the angle whose sine is x; range [−π/2, π/2].
Returns the angle whose cosine is x; range [0, π].
Returns the angle whose tangent is x; range (−π/2, π/2).
Trig functions are restricted to a one-to-one interval so they have inverses.
sin(arcsin x) = x and arcsin(sin θ) = θ only within the restricted range.
x(t) = A cos(ωt + φ); periodic motion with constant amplitude.
ω = 2π/T = 2πf; relates period and frequency to angular measure.
Distance between successive peaks of a wave; related to wave number k = 2π/λ.
R = (v₀² sin 2θ)/g; maximum at θ = 45°.
V(t) = V₀ sin(2πft + φ); sinusoidal model for alternating current.
Angles that share the same terminal side; differ by multiples of 360° (or 2π).
The positive acute angle (0°–90°) between the terminal side and the nearest x-axis.
Add or subtract 360° (2π) repeatedly until the result is in [0°, 360°).
Reference angle = 180° − θ (or π − θ).
Reference angle = θ − 180° (or θ − π).
Direction measured clockwise from north; e.g., N 30° E or a three-figure bearing like 030°.
a/sin A = b/sin B = c/sin C; relates sides and opposite angles.
c² = a² + b² − 2ab cos C; generalizes the Pythagorean theorem.
Determining position by measuring angles from known reference points.
Uses signals from ≥ 3 satellites and trilateration to find a receiver's position.
sin²θ + cos²θ = 1.
1 + tan²θ = sec²θ.
1 + cot²θ = csc²θ.
Transform one side of the equation to match the other using known identities.
Replace all trig functions with sin θ and cos θ to simplify.
sin(A+B) = sin A cos B + cos A sin B.
cos(A+B) = cos A cos B − sin A sin B.
tan(A+B) = (tan A + tan B)/(1 − tan A tan B).
sin(A−B) = sin A cos B − cos A sin B.
cos(A−B) = cos A cos B + sin A sin B.
sin 2θ = 2 sin θ cos θ.
cos 2θ = cos²θ − sin²θ = 2cos²θ − 1 = 1 − 2sin²θ.
tan 2θ = 2 tan θ/(1 − tan²θ).
sin(θ/2) = ±√((1 − cos θ)/2); sign by quadrant.
cos(θ/2) = ±√((1 + cos θ)/2); sign by quadrant.
(1/2)[sin(A+B) + sin(A−B)].
(1/2)[cos(A−B) + cos(A+B)].
(1/2)[cos(A−B) − cos(A+B)].
Add 2πn (or 360°n) to each solution for all solutions.
Add πn (or 180°n) for all solutions.
Substitute u = sin θ or cos θ, solve u² + bu + c = 0, then find angles.
Find the acute angle, then determine which quadrants give valid solutions.
Ensure all solutions are valid (e.g., sin θ = 2 has no solution).
y = A sin(B(t−C)) + D; models periodic phenomena.
(max − min)/2; half the range of oscillation.
(max + min)/2; the midline value.
Time for one complete cycle; B = 2π/period.
Horizontal adjustment C to align model with observed data.
Decomposes any periodic signal into a sum of sine and cosine waves at different frequencies.
Breaking a force F into components: F_x = F cos θ, F_y = F sin θ.
The additional phase lag needed to bring a system to instability; involves arctan.
Result of two waves with slightly different frequencies: amplitude varies at the difference frequency.
Z = R + jX; magnitude |Z| and phase angle θ = arctan(X/R).

flashcards.term: lim sin x / x as x→0
flashcards.definition: Equals 1; fundamental limit used in calculus proofs and L'Hôpital's rule.
Equals 0; another key limit for trig calculus.
d/dx[sin x] = cos x.
d/dx[cos x] = −sin x.
Replaces algebraic expressions with trig functions to simplify integrals.
d = a_{n+1} − a_n; constant for arithmetic sequences.
The middle term of three consecutive terms equals the average of the outer two.
Plotting a_n vs n for an arithmetic sequence gives a straight line with slope d.
r = a_{n+1}/a_n; constant for geometric sequences.
If |r| ≥ 1, the infinite geometric series diverges.
Σ_{k=m}^{n} a_k represents the sum from k=m to k=n.
Σ(ca_k + b_k) = cΣa_k + Σb_k.
A sum where consecutive terms cancel, leaving only the first and last parts.
Verify the statement for the smallest value (usually n = 1).
Assume the statement is true for n = k.
Prove the statement for n = k + 1 using the hypothesis.
Base case = first domino falls; inductive step = each domino knocks the next.
Assume the statement holds for ALL values ≤ k, then prove for k + 1.
C(n,k) = n!/(k!(n−k)!); the number of ways to choose k items from n.
(a+b)ⁿ = Σ C(n,k) a^(n−k) b^k; k = 0 to n.
Triangular array where each entry = sum of two entries above; row n gives coefficients of (a+b)ⁿ.
The (k+1)th term of (a+b)ⁿ is C(n,k) a^(n−k) b^k.
(a+b)² = a²+2ab+b²; (a+b)³ = a³+3a²b+3ab²+b³.
A = P(1 + r/n)^(nt); P = principal, r = rate, n = compoundings, t = years.
FV = P·[(1+r)ⁿ − 1]/r; sum of a geometric series of payments.
A = P(1 + rt); grows linearly (arithmetic sequence).
Time for a quantity to double; T = ln2/r for continuous growth.
PV = FV/(1+r)ⁿ; the current worth of a future amount.
P(X=k) = C(n,k)p^k(1−p)^(n−k); probability of exactly k successes in n trials.
E[X] = np; mean number of successes.
P(X=k) = (1−p)^(k−1)p; probability first success on trial k.
If event A has m outcomes and B has n outcomes, together they have m·n outcomes.
Σ P(X=k) = 1 (over all possible k).
lim_{x→c} f(x) = L means f(x) → L as x → c.
lim_{x→c⁻} f(x); the limit as x approaches c from the left.
lim_{x→c⁺} f(x); the limit as x approaches c from the right.
The limit exists iff left-hand limit = right-hand limit.
lim_{x→c} f(x) can differ from f(c); the limit depends only on nearby values.
If f is continuous at c, lim_{x→c} f(x) = f(c).
For 0/0 forms, factor numerator/denominator and cancel.
Multiply by conjugate to eliminate radicals (e.g., √a − √b).
lim(f + g) = lim f + lim g.
If g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ h(x) and lim g = lim h = L, then lim f = L.
lim_{x→c⁻} f(x): value f approaches as x → c from below.
lim_{x→c⁺} f(x): value f approaches as x → c from above.
Iff left-hand = right-hand limit (and both exist).
Occurs when left-hand and right-hand limits exist but are unequal.
At the boundary of a domain, only one one-sided limit is relevant.
f(c) defined, lim exists, and lim = f(c).
Limit exists but ≠ f(c) (or f(c) undefined); can be 'fixed' by redefining f(c).
Left and right limits both exist but are unequal.
lim_{x→c} f(x) = ±∞; vertical asymptote at x = c.
If f is continuous on [a,b] and N is between f(a) and f(b), then f(c)=N for some c in (a,b).
x = c where lim_{x→c} f(x) = ±∞.
y = L where lim_{x→±∞} f(x) = L.
Compare degrees of numerator and denominator to find HA.
Occurs when numerator degree = denominator degree + 1; found by polynomial division.
lim_{x→∞} f(x) describes the long-run behavior of f.
[f(b)−f(a)]/(b−a); slope of the secant line between (a,f(a)) and (b,f(b)).
lim_{h→0} [f(a+h)−f(a)]/h; slope of the tangent line at x = a.
[f(a+h)−f(a)]/h; the expression whose limit gives the derivative.
A line through two points on a curve; slope = average rate.
Touches the curve at one point; slope = instantaneous rate = derivative.
A function defined by different formulas on different intervals of its domain.
A point where the defining formula changes; check one-sided limits here.
At each boundary, verify left limit = right limit = function value.
Set parameters (like a, b) so pieces connect: left limit = right limit.
Absolute value |x| = {x, x≥0; −x, x<0} is a classic piecewise function.
Requires algebraic manipulation (factoring, rationalizing) before the limit can be evaluated.
If f continuous on [a,b] with f(a) and f(b) of opposite sign, then f has a zero in (a,b).
If g ≤ f ≤ h and lim g = lim h = L, then lim f = L.
Continuous functions on [a,b] are Riemann integrable — used in the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
If f continuous on [a,b] and differentiable on (a,b), then f'(c) = [f(b)−f(a)]/(b−a) for some c.

u10_l10.1.title: Derivative as Slope of Tangent Line

flashcards.definition: f'(a) = lim_{h→0} [f(a+h)−f(a)]/h; the instantaneous rate of change at a.
A line touching the curve at one point with slope equal to the derivative.
y − f(a) = f'(a)(x − a); point-slope form using the derivative.
As h → 0, the secant line through two nearby points becomes the tangent line.
A function is differentiable at a if f'(a) exists (the limit exists).
d/dx[xⁿ] = nxⁿ⁻¹; bring down the exponent, subtract 1.
d/dx[c] = 0; the derivative of any constant is zero.
d/dx[f + g] = f' + g'; differentiate term by term.
d/dx[cf(x)] = c·f'(x); constants factor out.
d/dx[eˣ] = eˣ; the exponential function is its own derivative.
A point where f'(x) = 0 or f'(x) is undefined; candidates for extrema.
If f' changes from + to − at c, local max. From − to +, local min.
If f'(c)=0 and f''(c)>0, local min. If f''(c)<0, local max.
1) Model the problem. 2) Find f'. 3) Set f'=0. 4) Check endpoints if closed interval.
On [a,b]: evaluate f at critical points and endpoints, compare values.
Approximation of area using rectangles: Σ f(xᵢ)Δx.
∫ₐᵇ f(x) dx = lim_{n→∞} Σ f(xᵢ)Δx; exact area under curve.
If F is an antiderivative of f, then ∫ₐᵇ f(x)dx = F(b) − F(a).
A function F such that F'(x) = f(x).
Use left or right endpoints of subintervals to evaluate f.
All calculus operations (diff, integration) are applied to functions.
d/dx[sin x]=cos x, ∫sin x dx = −cos x; identities simplify expressions.
d/dx[eˣ]=eˣ, d/dx[ln x]=1/x; model growth and decay.
f'(a) = lim_{h→0}[f(a+h)−f(a)]/h; limits define the derivative.
Approximating functions as infinite polynomials uses sequences and series skills.
Multiple choice (45 questions, 105 min) + Free response (6 questions, 90 min).
Some sections allow graphing calculators; others require algebraic/analytic methods.
AP free response requires written justification; 'because f'(x) changes sign' type reasoning.
Many AP questions give graphs of f, f', or f'' and ask about the others.
AP often gives real-world context; answers should include appropriate units.
A function or equation that describes a real-world phenomenon.
Statistical technique to find the best-fit curve for data (linear, quadratic, exponential, etc.).
Choose the function type based on data shape: linear (constant rate), quadratic (parabolic), exponential (growth/decay), trig (periodic).
Compare predicted vs actual values to assess model quality.
Models may not be reliable far beyond the range of data used to build them.
Continuous everywhere, smooth graphs, degree determines end behavior and max number of zeros.
Find asymptotes, holes, intercepts; domain excludes zeros of denominator.
Exponential: rapid growth/decay. Logarithmic: its inverse. Both critical in calculus.
Unit circle values, identities, and solving equations — all used extensively in calculus.
Limits → derivatives → integrals. Strong precalculus skills make calculus manageable.
